loc: Amygdala, hippocampus, anterior insula (co-generator)
notes: Most common MTLE aura; rising sensation from epigastrium to chest/throat; anterior insular co-involvement confirmed by SEEG; can arise from insula alone
cite: Van Buren, Epilepsia 1963; Henkel et al., Epilepsia 2002; Palmini & Gloor 1992

loc: Basolateral amygdala (primary); ACC and anterior insula as co-generators
notes: Ictal fear; direct amygdala stimulation elicits fear (Gloor); also from ACC and insula; intense, sudden, objectless fear; autonomic accompaniments common
cite: Gloor, Brain 1990; Feindel & Penfield 1954; Wieser 2004

loc: Hippocampus, rhinal cortex (entorhinal + perirhinal cortex)
notes: Familiarity illusion; more common from right mesial temporal; SEEG Bancaud confirmed rhinal cortex is key substrate (not purely hippocampal); Bartolomei stimulation study of rhinal cortex critical
cite: Bancaud et al., Brain 1994; Bartolomei et al., Neurology 2004

loc: Hippocampus, entorhinal cortex
notes: Strangeness/unreality; unfamiliarity illusion; overlaps with derealization; less common than deja vu; may co-occur with deja vu as oscillating familiarity state
cite: Bancaud et al., Brain 1994; Gloor 1990

loc: Amygdala (primary), piriform cortex, uncus; OFC as secondary
notes: Classic Jacksonian uncinate fit; typically unpleasant or burning; SEEG: piriform-amygdalar origin; OFC stimulation also elicits olfactory hallucination (may be more pleasant quality)
cite: Jackson, Brain 1889; Daly 1958; Murai et al. 1997

loc: Amygdala (input pathway); primary substrate = anterior insula + parietal operculum (area 43)
notes: Metallic or unpleasant taste hallucination; amygdala receives gustatory input but primary cortical substrate is insular-opercular; SEEG: Hausser-Hauw & Bancaud established insular-opercular primacy
cite: Hausser-Hauw & Bancaud, Brain 1987; Siegel 1999; Isnard et al. 2000

loc: Lateral temporal neocortex (STG/MTG) + hippocampus co-activation required
notes: Vivid autobiographical replay; Penfield interpretive cortex; SEEG shows lateral temporal neocortex + hippocampal co-activation required; cannot arise from hippocampus alone; right temporal more common for experiential auras
cite: Penfield & Perot, Brain 1963; Gloor 1990; Bancaud et al. 1994

sign: Motionless stare / ictal behavioral arrest (hypomotor onset)
loc: Mesial temporal (bilateral hippocampal involvement for impaired consciousness)
notes: Classic early MTLE sign; impaired consciousness with motionless stare; bilateral hippocampal spread required for unconsciousness; duration >30s helps distinguish from absence; highly sensitive for TLE but non-lateralizing
cite: Kotagal et al., Epilepsy Res 1995; Bleasel et al., Epilepsia 1997

sign: Oral automatisms (lip smacking, chewing, swallowing, licking)
loc: Mesial temporal (amygdalo-hippocampal complex) -> orbitofrontal propagation
notes: Among most studied automatisms; ipsilateral to EZ; oral > manual in pure MTLE; SEEG confirms amygdalo-hippocampal origin with OFC propagation; chewing rate may encode propagation speed
cite: Kotagal et al., Epilepsy Res 1995; Bleasel et al., Epilepsia 1997; Williamson 1985

sign: Unilateral manual automatisms (ipsilateral fumbling, picking, patting)
loc: Mesial temporal; contralateral limb may simultaneously show dystonia
notes: Ipsilateral hand automatisms; KEY: automatisms ipsilateral while contralateral arm often shows dystonic posturing (complementary sign); combined AP sign = highest specificity; complex unilateral hand movements
cite: Kotagal et al., Epilepsy Res 1995; Bleasel et al., Epilepsia 1997

lat: CONTRALATERAL - HIGHEST SPECIFICITY SIGN IN TLE
loc: EZ in ipsilateral mesial temporal; mechanism via temporal->subthalamic->basal ganglia loop producing contralateral inhibition/dystonia
notes: GOLD STANDARD lateralizer; tonic writhing/dystonic posture of contralateral limb; BG-mediated via subthalamic pathway; seminal Kotagal 1989 paper; arm > leg; combination with ipsilateral automatisms (AP sign) approaches 100% PPV
cite: Kotagal et al., Neurology 1989; Marks & Laxer 1998; Bleasel et al. 1997; O'Brien et al., Brain 1996

sign: Late forced/tonic head version (>30s, sustained, contralateral)
loc: Temporal onset -> frontal lobe propagation (FEF, Brodmann 8 / premotor cortex)
notes: KEY DISTINCTION: late (>30s), forced, sustained tonic deviation = CONTRALATERAL; reflects seizure spread to FEF; immediately pre-GTCS versive turn is most reliable; Wyllie 1986 seminal paper
cite: Wyllie et al., Neurology 1986; Robillard et al., Neurology 1983; Ochs et al., Neurology 1984

sign: Early non-forced head turning (<30s, ipsilateral, exploratory)
loc: Temporal lobe ictal onset zone (orienting/searching behavior)
notes: CRITICAL DISTINCTION: early (<30s), non-forced, exploratory head turn toward EZ side; ictal orienting behavior; lower specificity than late versive; must be distinguished from late tonic version; Chee group confirmation
cite: Wyllie et al., Neurology 1986; Chee et al., Epilepsia 1993; Robillard et al. 1983

sign: Figure-of-4 sign (extended arm + flexed arm asymmetric tonic)
lat: CONTRALATERAL - extended arm = contralateral to EZ
loc: Motor cortex propagation from temporal or frontal EZ; asymmetric bilateral motor involvement
notes: Extended arm = contralateral to EZ; observed during tonic phase of GTCS evolution; very high specificity; distinct from bilateral asymmetric tonic seizure (BATS); both arms form figure-4 posture; landmark Kotagal paper
cite: Kotagal et al., Epilepsia 2000; Vendrame et al., Epilepsy Behav 2010

loc: Mesial temporal EZ (ipsilateral); BG loop produces contralateral dystonia
notes: MOST POWERFUL TEMPORAL LATERALIZER: ipsilateral hand automatisms + contralateral dystonic arm posturing simultaneously; each component individually lateralizing; combination approaches 100% specificity for mesial temporal EZ; Kotagal foundational observation
cite: Kotagal et al., Neurology 1989; Bleasel et al. 1997; O'Brien et al. 1996

loc: Dominant mesial temporal; insular-temporal network; orbitofrontal component
notes: Spitting as ictal behavior; predominantly from dominant (left) temporal; OPPOSITE lateralization to ictus emeticus; insular-temporal network; rare but highly specific when observed
cite: Kellinghaus et al., Epilepsia 2003; Devinsky et al. 1995

loc: Right mesial temporal, right temporal neocortex
notes: Strongly non-dominant (right) hemisphere; OPPOSITE lateralization to ictal spitting; temporal-insular-brainstem vomiting center network; rare but highly specific; Devinsky seminal paper
cite: Devinsky et al., Neurology 1995; Kramer et al., Seizure 2001

loc: Temporal lobe propagation; possible occipital contribution
notes: Controversial lateralization; majority of studies suggest ipsilateral; some SDE/SEEG data show contralateral in minority; blink rate analysis may refine; requires careful video analysis; not currently used as reliable lateralizer
cite: Theodore et al. 1994; Baykan et al. 2002; Elger et al. 1992

lat: Right > bilateral (right insular-temporal for cardiac effects)
loc: Right temporal lobe; right insular cortex (cardiac sympathetic)
notes: Very common but non-specific for lateralization; right insular-temporal predominant for sympathetic cardiac effects; limited lateralizing value alone; combined with other signs increases utility; ictal bradycardia (below) is more specific
cite: Oppenheimer et al. 1992; Nei et al. 2009; Seeck et al. 1999

loc: Right posterior insular cortex; right temporal lobe
notes: Rare but clinically critical; RIGHT insular cortex dominance for parasympathetic-mediated bradycardia; SUDEP mechanism; SEEG confirms right insular specificity; mandatory cardiac monitoring in all presurgical evaluations
cite: Oppenheimer et al. 1992; Devinsky et al. 2004; Nashef et al. 2007

loc: Right temporal-insular junction; sympathetic pathway
notes: Sympathetic pilomotor response; ipsilateral to EZ; right-sided predilection (sympathetic right insular); often accompanies fear aura or epigastric aura; Loddenkemper key reference
cite: Loddenkemper et al., Epilepsia 2001; Remillard et al. 1983

loc: Non-dominant (right) mesial temporal; unilateral mesial temporal involvement only
notes: Automatisms with preserved responsiveness implies non-dominant hemisphere; bilateral mesial temporal spread disrupts consciousness; right MTLE more often preserves awareness; critical for lateralization in non-dominant TLE
cite: Gloor 1990; Kotagal et al. 1992; Williamson 1993

lat: IPSILATERAL (ipsilateral hand wipes ipsilateral side of nose)
loc: Temporal lobe (ipsilateral EZ); mechanism uncertain
notes: Ipsilateral hand wipes ipsilateral nose in postictal period; one of most reliable postictal lateralizing signs; two independent seminal papers simultaneously in 1998 (Hirsch and Leutmezer); begins within 1-2 min postictal
cite: Hirsch et al., Neurology 1998; Leutmezer et al., Neurology 1998

loc: Left (dominant) temporal lobe
notes: Highly reliable dominant hemisphere lateralizer; distinguishes left from right TLE; duration proportional to EZ proximity to Wernicke area; concordant with Wada testing and language mapping; most useful postictal sign
cite: Gabr et al., Ann Neurol 1989; Devinsky et al. 1993

sign: Ictal aphasia / speech arrest during seizure
loc: Dominant temporal (Wernicke) or dominant SMA (if arrest type)
notes: Ictal language disturbance; dominant hemisphere; posterior type = Wernicke involvement; anterior type = SMA arrest; must distinguish temporal aphasia (with paraphasia) from SMA arrest (pure arrest without language content); Gabr and Penfield key
cite: Penfield & Roberts 1959; Gabr et al. 1989; Luders et al. 1987

loc: Temporal lobe, anterior cingulate cortex, hypothalamic hamartoma (limbic network)
notes: Extremely rare; ictal crying with tears; can coexist with gelastic seizures (esp. with hypothalamic hamartoma); temporal and ACC origins documented by SEEG (Luciano); always consider hypothalamic hamartoma on MRI
cite: Luciano et al., Epilepsia 1993; Satow et al. 2003; Kahane et al. 1994

loc: Temporal neocortex, OFC-temporal network (non-hamartoma origin)
notes: Must rule out hypothalamic hamartoma by MRI; non-hamartoma gelastics involve OFC-temporal-cingulate network (Arroyo, Kahane SEEG); temporal gelastics often have associated automatisms; rare
cite: Berkovic et al., Ann Neurol 1988; Arroyo et al. 1993; Kahane et al. 1994

sub: Lateral Temporal Neocortex (STG / MTG / ITG / Temporal Pole)
sign: Simple auditory aura (tones, buzzing, hissing, roaring)
lat: Contralateral (right for non-verbal; left/dominant for language sounds)
loc: Primary auditory cortex (Heschl gyri, planum temporale, Brodmann 41/42)
notes: Simple unilateral tonal/noise aura = contralateral primary auditory cortex; right STG for non-verbal tones; left for speech sounds; distinct from complex formed auditory hallucinations (higher-order auditory areas); cortical stimulation of Heschl gyrus reproduces
cite: Penfield & Jasper 1954; Bancaud et al. 1994; Wieser et al. 1993

sub: Lateral Temporal Neocortex (STG / MTG / ITG / Temporal Pole)
sign: Complex auditory hallucinations (formed music, songs, melodies)
loc: Right superior temporal gyrus (music processing), anterior STG, Brodmann 22
notes: Formed music hallucinations: right hemisphere predominant; left hemisphere for verbal/linguistic content; Penfield interpretive cortex confirmed by modern SDE; music vs. speech distinction is clinically useful hemispheric localizer
cite: Penfield & Perot, Brain 1963; Gordon 1997; Bancaud 1994

sub: Lateral Temporal Neocortex (STG / MTG / ITG / Temporal Pole)
loc: Left STG, Wernicke area (Brodmann 22/42)
notes: Formed verbal hallucinations (hearing voices/speech); dominant hemisphere; Penfield interpretive cortex; cortical stimulation at left STG produces language-content hallucinations; clinically relevant differential from psychotic disorders
cite: Penfield & Perot, Brain 1963; Bancaud 1994

sub: Lateral Temporal Neocortex (STG / MTG / ITG / Temporal Pole)
sign: Auditory illusions (sounds distorted in quality, loudness, or distance)
loc: STG, auditory association cortex (Brodmann 22/39)
notes: Qualitative distortion of existing sounds (not a new perception); right non-dominant for non-verbal distortions; distinct from hallucination: distorted existing sound vs. new perception; SDE evidence from lateral temporal mapping
cite: Bancaud et al. 1994; Penfield & Perot 1963

sub: Lateral Temporal Neocortex (STG / MTG / ITG / Temporal Pole)
loc: Temporo-parietal junction (posterior STG / angular gyrus); posterior insula (PIVC) co-activation
notes: True rotatory vestibular vertigo; must distinguish from dizziness; TPJ and posterior insula co-activation by SEEG; cortical stimulation at posterior STG/TPJ elicits vestibular sensations
cite: Smith 1960; Brandt et al. 1999; Kahane et al. 2003; Isnard et al. 2000

sub: Lateral Temporal Neocortex (STG / MTG / ITG / Temporal Pole)
loc: Right inferior temporal, temporal-occipital junction, extrastriate regions
notes: Objects appear abnormally small (micropsia) or large (macropsia); right hemisphere dominant for visuospatial processing; Alice in Wonderland syndrome; temporal-occipital ventral stream involvement
cite: Penfield & Rasmussen 1950; Lippman 1952; Engel 1989

sub: Lateral Temporal Neocortex (STG / MTG / ITG / Temporal Pole)
loc: Right fusiform, inferior temporal, temporal-occipital junction
notes: Distortion of visual form/shape (objects appear twisted/deformed); right temporal-occipital (ventral stream); distinct from micropsia (shape vs. size distortion); fusiform cortex role in form perception
cite: Penfield & Rasmussen 1950; Salanova et al. 1992

sub: Lateral Temporal Neocortex (STG / MTG / ITG / Temporal Pole)
sign: Formed semantic visual hallucinations (faces, people, scenes, objects)
loc: Fusiform gyrus (right > left for faces), inferior temporal gyrus, lateral temporal-occipital junction
notes: EXPERT DISTINCTION: semantic visual content (faces, people, objects) = lateral temporal-occipital ventral stream; DISTINCT from elementary occipital visual phenomena (phosphenes/geometry = V1/V2); presence of faces/people implies temporal-occipital NOT primary occipital origin
cite: Penfield & Perot 1963; Gloor 1990; Zeki 1993

sub: Lateral Temporal Neocortex (STG / MTG / ITG / Temporal Pole)
loc: Left STG, Wernicke area (Brodmann 22)
notes: Ictal word substitution errors (paraphasia) during active speech; highly reliable dominant hemisphere lateralizer; also manifests postictal; Gabr 1989 key clinical study
cite: Gabr et al., Ann Neurol 1989; Dinner et al. 1987

sub: Lateral Temporal Neocortex (STG / MTG / ITG / Temporal Pole)
loc: Lateral temporal neocortex (STG/MTG) + hippocampus (co-activation required)
notes: Jackson dreamy state; altered reality sense, panoramic memories, deja vu + visual experiential; SEEG: lateral temporal neocortex + hippocampal co-activation required; cannot arise from hippocampus alone; may involve right hemisphere more
cite: Jackson 1889; Penfield & Perot 1963; Gloor 1990

sub: Primary Motor Cortex (M1, precentral gyrus, Brodmann 4)
loc: Primary motor cortex M1, somatotopic representation (hand > face > leg)
notes: Contralateral rhythmic clonic jerks; somatotopic distribution encodes location within M1; hand knob (omega-shaped precentral knob) most common; gold standard contralateral motor sign; Penfield homuncular mapping foundational
cite: Jackson 1873; Penfield & Jasper 1954; Rasmussen 1975

sub: Primary Motor Cortex (M1, precentral gyrus, Brodmann 4)
loc: Sequential somatotopic propagation through M1 (e.g., thumb->hand->arm->face->leg)
notes: MOST SPECIFIC M1 SIGN; sequential march of clonic activity through somatotopic map; direction of march encodes cortical spread; Jackson original description (1873); propagation can be used to infer cortical organization; highest specificity of all frontal signs
cite: Jackson 1873; Penfield & Jasper 1954; Wieser et al. 1993

sub: Primary Motor Cortex (M1, precentral gyrus, Brodmann 4)
loc: Primary motor cortex (cortical spreading depression or inhibition post-ictally)
notes: Rare but highly specific for M1 involvement; duration proportional to seizure severity; mechanism: cortical spreading depression or GABAergic post-ictal inhibition of M1; must be distinguished from ictal Todd (true weakness vs. postictal)
cite: Todd 1849; Bleasel et al. 1997; Gallmetzer et al. 2004

sub: Primary Motor Cortex (M1, precentral gyrus, Brodmann 4)
loc: M1 face area (lateral M1, inferior precentral gyrus, Brodmann 4 face area)
notes: Hemifacial clonic activity; contralateral facial motor area; must distinguish from benign rolandic epilepsy pattern; may involve adjacent opercular/premotor areas
cite: Penfield & Jasper 1954; Rasmussen 1975

sub: Primary Motor Cortex (M1, precentral gyrus, Brodmann 4)
loc: M1 hand area (precentral knob, omega-shaped fold, Brodmann 4 hand area)
notes: Focal finger/hand clonic jerking; omega-shaped precentral knob on MRI identifies hand area precisely; most commonly involved M1 zone; Yousry 1997 MRI identification of precentral knob
cite: Penfield & Jasper 1954; Yousry et al. 1997 (precentral knob MRI)

sub: Primary Motor Cortex (M1, precentral gyrus, Brodmann 4)
lat: IPSILATERAL (toward EZ side - OPPOSITE of ictal versive)
loc: FEF or M1 postictally inhibited; gaze drifts toward inhibited hemisphere
notes: OPPOSITE direction to ictal versive deviation; postictal gaze toward EZ side; reflects post-ictal inhibition of contralateral FEF; CRITICAL to distinguish from ictal versive (away from EZ); must specify timing carefully
cite: Penfield & Jasper 1954; Ochs et al. 1984

sign: Bilateral asymmetric tonic posturing (fencing posture / BATS)
lat: Extended arm = CONTRALATERAL to EZ; ipsilateral arm flexed
loc: SMA (mesial premotor, Brodmann 6 medial; superior frontal gyrus mesial surface)
notes: HALLMARK SMA SEIZURE; bilateral tonic with asymmetry; contralateral arm extends toward fencing direction; ipsilateral arm may flex; also seen with temporal->motor propagation; bilateral involvement confirms SMA; often brief with rapid offset
cite: Lim et al., Electroencephalogr Clin Neurophysiol 1994; Salanova et al., Epilepsia 1995; Williamson et al. 1985

sign: M2E automatism (mouth-to-hand: ipsilateral hand moves to mouth)
loc: SMA / pre-SMA (mesial frontal, anterior Brodmann 6)
notes: EXPERT SIGN (Bonini/Marseille SEEG group): ipsilateral hand raises toward mouth; DISTINCT from simple oral automatisms; implies SMA or mesial frontal onset; hand is on SAME SIDE as EZ; elegant localizer from Chauvel school of SEEG semiology
cite: Bonini et al., Epilepsia 2014; Lim et al. 1996; Bartolomei et al. 2010

sign: Chapeau de gendarme sign (bilateral forced lip-corner retraction)
loc: SMA / pre-SMA and anterior cingulate cortex (bilateral mesial frontal activation)
notes: RARE EXPERT SIGN (Paris SEEG school): bilateral forced retraction of lip corners (NOT a pout - corners retract sideways); resembles brim of French gendarme hat; Bancaud & Talairach; bilateral mesial frontal; subtle but highly specific for mesial frontal/SMA involvement
cite: Bancaud & Talairach, Adv Neurol 1992; Geier et al. 1977; Luders & Noachtar 2000

sign: Ictal pout (perioral dystonic protrusion/pursing - distinct from chapeau)
loc: SMA perioral representation, pre-SMA mesial frontal
notes: Perioral dystonic protrusion/pursing (lips pushed FORWARD = pout); DISTINCT from chapeau (which has lip CORNERS retracted sideways); SMA perioral homuncular involvement; may precede or coexist with chapeau sign; both reflect bilateral mesial frontal activation
cite: Bancaud & Talairach 1992; Luders & Noachtar 2000

loc: Bilateral SMA (laryngeal representation), bilateral laryngeal M1
notes: Non-linguistic tonic vocalization; involves bilateral SMA and bilateral laryngeal M1; NOT lateralizing per Wyllie/Wieser; some data suggest association with secondary generalization; distinct from language-based ictal speech
cite: Wieser et al. 1985; Wyllie et al. 1986; Lim et al. 1994

sign: Speech arrest / ictal mutism (dominant SMA, inhibitory)
loc: Dominant SMA (left in right-handers); possibly pre-SMA
notes: Inability to produce speech during otherwise alert seizure; dominant hemisphere SMA; inhibitory mechanism via dominant mesial frontal; SEEG-confirmed; DISTINCT from Wernicke-type aphasia: SMA arrest = pure arrest without language errors; temporal = paraphasia
cite: Penfield & Roberts 1959; Lim et al. 1994; Luders et al. 1987

sign: Bilateral leg elevation with cycling/pedaling movements
loc: SMA/mesial frontal (leg representation), anterior cingulate cortex
notes: Cycling bicycle leg movements; bilateral leg elevation; classic NFLE hypermotor feature; SMA and cingulate motor area bilateral activation; common in nocturnal frontal seizures; should prompt SEEG if scalp EEG unrevealing
cite: Williamson et al. 1985; Salanova et al. 1995; Provini et al. 1999

sign: Nocturnal brief stereotyped seizures (rapid onset/offset, minimal postictal)
loc: Mesial frontal network (SMA, cingulate, OFC) - NFLE signature
notes: Clinical signature of nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (NFLE); brief (<2 min); rapid return to baseline; minimal postictal state; stereotyped; SEEG: mesial frontal predominant; frequently misdiagnosed as parasomnias (REM behavior disorder, sleepwalking)
cite: Provini et al., Neurology 1999; Scheffer et al. 1995; Plazzi et al. 1996

loc: Orbitofrontal cortex, mesial frontal, anterior cingulate, insular cortex (OFC-cingulate network)
notes: SEEG-validated (Chauvel/McGonigal/Bonini): hypermotor behaviors from OFC, mesial frontal, cingulate; often misdiagnosed as PNES; explosive onset with complex motor behavior; Ryvlin 2006 SEEG showed insular origin also; OFC-cingulate-insular network is key substrate
cite: Williamson et al. 1985; Bancaud & Talairach 1992; Vaugier et al. 2009; Bonini et al. 2014; Ryvlin et al. 2006

loc: Orbitofrontal cortex (Brodmann 11, secondary olfactory cortex); piriform cortex input
notes: OFC olfactory hallucinations may be more pleasant/neutral quality vs. amygdala-based uncinate fits (typically unpleasant/burning); OFC is secondary olfactory cortex receiving piriform and amygdala input; SEEG confirms OFC as secondary olfactory processing area
cite: Daly 1958; Murai et al. 1997; Isnard et al. 2000

loc: Orbitofrontal cortex, piriform cortex
notes: Repetitive sniffing movements; OFC/piriform involvement; can accompany olfactory auras; SEEG-confirmed OFC origin; also described with insular involvement; rare but characteristically orbitofrontal
cite: Bancaud & Talairach 1992

loc: Orbitofrontal, anterior temporal, amygdala (OFC-limbic loop)
notes: Stereotyped complex behaviors appearing purposeful but contextually inappropriate; OFC involvement typical; SEEG shows OFC-amygdala propagation; often nocturnal; subtle and easily misdiagnosed as parasomnia or PNES
cite: Bancaud & Talairach 1992; Williamson et al. 1985

sign: Hypermotor agitated behavior (ACC-dominant type with emotional coloring)
loc: Anterior cingulate cortex (Brodmann 24), mid-cingulate motor area (24-prime)
notes: ACC-origin hypermotor seizures have more prominent emotional/autonomic coloring vs. SMA hypermotor; SEEG (Devinsky, Bartolomei): ACC involvement produces emotional + motor behaviors; bilateral nature explains non-lateralizing quality
cite: Devinsky et al., Brain 1995; Bancaud & Talairach 1992; Bartolomei et al. 2010

loc: Mid-cingulate cortex (Brodmann 24-prime), mesial frontal SMA, orbitofrontal
notes: Ictal pelvic thrusting or sexual automatisms; frontal/cingulate predominant; SEEG: Bancaud & Talairach described frontal origin; NFLE common context; temporal origin also possible; must distinguish from PNES
cite: Bancaud & Talairach 1992; Spencer et al. 1983; Remillard et al. 1983

loc: ACC (area 24), OFC (frontal gelastic network)
notes: Non-hypothalamic gelastic seizures from ACC or OFC; SEEG: Arroyo showed frontal network participation; MRI MUST rule out hypothalamic hamartoma first; ACC gelastics often accompanied by hypermotor features; ACC stimulation produces laughter in humans
cite: Arroyo et al. 1993; Bancaud & Talairach 1992

loc: ACC, non-dominant prefrontal cortex
notes: Involuntary ictal obscene vocalizations; extremely rare; non-dominant prefrontal/ACC; SEEG-described in case reports (Biraben); must distinguish from Tourette syndrome; frontal disinhibition mechanism; unique electroclinical sign
cite: Biraben et al. 2001

sign: Forced contralateral tonic head/eye version (late, sustained)
loc: Frontal eye field (FEF, Brodmann 8); also from temporal or occipital via propagation
notes: Forced, sustained, tonic deviation of eyes and head AWAY from EZ; late timing (>30s) and forced/tonic quality = contralateral; must strictly distinguish from early non-forced turn (ipsilateral); propagated from temporal or direct FEF origin
cite: Penfield & Jasper 1954; Wyllie et al. 1986; Chee et al. 1993; Salanova et al. 1995

sign: Ictal negative motor phenomenon (inhibitory arrest of contralateral limb)
loc: Negative motor cortex (Brodmann 6 inferior, lateral premotor; area 44/45 pars triangularis)
notes: Inability to move contralateral limb during otherwise alert state; Luders/Cleveland group defined negative motor cortex by cortical stimulation; SEEG-confirmed; important to recognize and distinguish from positive motor phenomena; critical region near Broca in dominant hemisphere
cite: Luders et al. 1995; Dinner et al. 1991; Penfield & Jasper 1954

sub: Primary Somatosensory Cortex (S1, postcentral gyrus, Brodmann 3/1/2)
sign: Somatosensory aura (tingling, numbness, paresthesia - contralateral, somatotopic)
loc: S1 postcentral gyrus (Brodmann 3/1/2); somatotopically organized (face>hand>leg medially)
notes: Classic parietal aura; contralateral tingling/numbness in somatotopic distribution; face and hand most common; cortical stimulation confirms somatotopic map; can mimic TIA; seizure onset rapid; paresthesia quality (not pure numbness alone)
cite: Salanova et al., Brain 1995; Kim et al., Epilepsia 2004; Penfield & Jasper 1954

sub: Primary Somatosensory Cortex (S1, postcentral gyrus, Brodmann 3/1/2)
sign: Somatosensory Jacksonian march (sensory spread through somatotopic map)
loc: Sequential somatotopic sensory propagation through S1
notes: Sequential march of sensory symptoms through S1 somatotopic map; sensory equivalent of Jacksonian motor march; highly specific for S1; propagation direction identifies cortical spread; very rarely bilateral (suggests SMA or secondary spread)
cite: Salanova et al. 1995; Kim et al. 2004; Penfield & Jasper 1954

sub: Primary Somatosensory Cortex (S1, postcentral gyrus, Brodmann 3/1/2)
loc: S1 (rare pure pain); posterior insula and parietal operculum (more common for painful quality)
notes: RARE SIGN; painful seizure aura; burning or sharp quality; Young & Blume 1983 first series; S1 stimulation can produce painful sensations; insular cortex is key for pain processing; can mimic acute pain syndromes leading to diagnostic delay
cite: Young & Blume, Can J Neurol Sci 1983; Ostrowsky et al. 2002; Salanova et al. 1995

sub: Primary Somatosensory Cortex (S1, postcentral gyrus, Brodmann 3/1/2)
loc: Paracentral lobule (S1/M1 mesial genital area); also upper bank of Sylvian fissure (perineum)
notes: Genital/perineal sensory aura; paracentral lobule (mesial hemispheric S1/M1); SEEG-confirmed; can precede hypermotor behaviors in mesial frontal seizures; must also consider cingulate origin; important to ask about specifically as patients often reluctant to volunteer
cite: Spencer et al. 1983; Williamson 1992; Remillard et al. 1983

sub: Primary Somatosensory Cortex (S1, postcentral gyrus, Brodmann 3/1/2)
loc: S1 postcentral gyrus (vibratory perception column, Meissner/Pacinian pathway)
notes: RARE EXPERT SIGN: vibratory or buzzing quality to contralateral somatosensory aura; S1 parietal origin; distinct from tingling (different quality: like vibrating phone or electric current passing through limb); differentiates from simple numbness
cite: Salanova et al. 1995; Young & Blume 1983

sub: Primary Somatosensory Cortex (S1, postcentral gyrus, Brodmann 3/1/2)
loc: S1/S2, superior parietal lobule, SSMA
notes: Sensation of limb movement when limb is actually stationary; S1/SPL or SSMA involvement; SEEG-confirmed (Mauguiere, Lyon group); parietal kinesthetic cortex; may be confused with vertigo or SMA-type motor phenomena
cite: Mauguiere & Courjon 1983; Salanova et al. 1995

loc: Temporo-parietal junction (TPJ), right angular gyrus (Brodmann 39)
notes: RARE EXPERT SIGN: sensation of leaving body or observing self from external vantage; RIGHT TPJ dominant; Blanke 2002 Nature: focal electrical stimulation of right angular gyrus produces OBE; SEEG: right TPJ focal onset documented; autoscopy (passive) vs. heautoscopy (projected self acts independently)
cite: Blanke et al., Nature 2002; Blanke et al., Brain 2004; Devinsky et al. 1989

loc: Superior parietal lobule, precuneus; possibly posterior insula or SMA
notes: Sense of self rotating or being rotated; egocentric vestibular-proprioceptive integration; SPL/precuneus involvement; can also be SMA (whole-body rotation); Blanke group confirmed TPJ/SPL origin; distinguish from peripheral vertigo
cite: Blanke et al. 2002; Salanova et al. 1995; Kahane et al. 2003

loc: Right superior parietal lobule (SPL, Brodmann 7), right angular/supramarginal gyri (IPL)
notes: Ictal spatial disorientation or neglect; right parietal dominant for spatial attention; postictal neglect may be more prominent and easier to test; requires formal bedside testing; SEEG-confirmed by Kim and Salanova groups
cite: Salanova et al., Brain 1995; Kim et al., Epilepsia 2004; Critchley 1953

loc: Left (dominant) SPL or corpus callosum involvement; also SMA (motor alien hand)
notes: RARE: Feeling that contralateral limb is foreign or not own; PARIETAL alien limb = limb does not feel mine (sensory); SMA alien hand = limb moves on its own (motor); CRITICAL distinction; callosal involvement may contribute to either type
cite: Geschwind et al. 1995; Della Sala et al. 1991; Goldberg et al. 1981

loc: Parietal operculum (Brodmann 43, S2 zone), Sylvian operculum
notes: Parietal operculum (area 43/S2) is secondary somatosensory and gustatory cortex; metallic or bitter taste; Hausser-Hauw & Bancaud SEEG paper classified parietal opercular seizures; opercular and insular cortex together form complete gustatory substrate
cite: Hausser-Hauw & Bancaud, Brain 1987; Siegel et al. 1999

sign: Elementary visual phenomena (phosphenes, flickering lights, geometric forms, fortification spectra)
loc: V1/V2 (calcarine sulcus and pericalcarine cortex, Brodmann 17/18)
notes: Classic occipital aura; flickering colored lights, phosphenes, fortification spectra in contralateral hemifield; DISTINGUISH from migraine aura: seizure aura faster onset (seconds not minutes), more multicolored, multidirectional movement; Panayiotopoulos and Salanova landmark references
cite: Panayiotopoulos, JNNP 1999; Salanova et al., Brain 1992; Williamson et al., Ann Neurol 1992

lat: Contralateral hemifield OR bilateral (if bilateral occipital or spread)
loc: V1 (bilateral or unilateral calcarine)
notes: Unilateral calcarine -> contralateral hemifield loss; bilateral V1 or rapid propagation -> complete blindness; postictal visual loss highly localizing for occipital EZ; must distinguish ictal positive (phosphenes) from negative (blindness) phenomena
cite: Salanova et al. 1992; Williamson et al. 1992; Panayiotopoulos 1999

sign: Colored visual elementary hallucinations (yellow, red, blue, multicolored)
loc: V4 (lingual-fusiform color processing area, ventral V2/V3 pericalcarine); V2 pericalcarine
notes: Colored (multicolored, yellow, red) elementary visual phenomena strongly suggest occipital; V4 color area in lingual/fusiform region; more specific than gray/white phosphenes; Zeki color area V4 (ventral occipital)
cite: Zeki 1991; Salanova et al. 1992; Panayiotopoulos 1999

loc: Occipital eye field (area 19/18, posterior parieto-occipital) activating FEF via propagation
notes: Contralateral tonic eye deviation during occipital seizures; occipital drives FEF via posterior parieto-occipital pathway; direction identical to frontal FEF but mechanism different; SEEG shows occipital->parietal->FEF propagation
cite: Salanova et al. 1992; Kaplan & Lesser 1989

lat: Fast phase CONTRALATERAL to EZ (jerk nystagmus toward non-EZ side)
loc: Occipital cortex or FEF; fast phase driven by ipsilateral cortical activation
notes: Ictal nystagmus; fast phase contralateral to EZ in occipital seizures; Kaplan & Lesser seminal analysis; also seen in frontal/parietal seizures; combined EEG-eye movement analysis required for reliable interpretation
cite: Kaplan & Lesser 1989; Salanova et al. 1992

loc: Occipital cortex; cortical spreading depression mechanism
notes: More common in occipital epilepsy than other focal epilepsies; must distinguish from migraine; key differential: seizure aura faster (<5 min), more colored/multicolored, multidirectional movement vs. migraine (slower, unidirectional, gray/white); postictal headache may be prolonged
cite: Panayiotopoulos 1999; Williamson et al. 1992

loc: Occipital cortex (V1), calcarine
notes: Postictal visual field defect in contralateral hemifield; highly specific for occipital EZ; duration minutes to hours; postictal visual field testing by confrontation is critical; distinct from ictal amaurosis; Panayiotopoulos and Williamson groups
cite: Panayiotopoulos 1999; Williamson et al. 1992; Salanova et al. 1992

sign: Moving visual phenomena (motion-specific: moving dots, patterns, streams - without form)
loc: V5/MT complex (lateral occipital-temporal junction, Brodmann 19/37 boundary, lateral occipital sulcus)
notes: EXPERT DISTINCTION: motion-specific phenomena WITHOUT form = V5/MT area; stationary colored patterns = V1/V4 calcarine; Zeki motion area; cortical stimulation of V5/MT produces motion perception; movement quality without form content implies extrastriate not primary occipital
cite: Zeki 1991; Williamson et al. 1992; Salanova et al. 1992

sign: Geometric formed visual hallucinations (grids, kaleidoscope, checkerboards - without semantic content)
loc: V2-V4 (pericalcarine cortex, lingual/fusiform, Brodmann 18/19 ventral)
notes: Formed geometric patterns without recognizable objects = extrastriate V2-V4; DISTINCT from semantic hallucinations (faces/objects = temporal-occipital); distinction based on content: purely geometric (V2-V4) vs. semantic faces/objects (inferior temporal-occipital)
cite: Salanova et al. 1992; Williamson et al. 1992; Penfield & Jasper 1954

sign: Laryngeal / throat constriction (tightening, strangling, pressure sensation)
lat: Non-lateralizing (bilateral; slight dominant tendency per some SEEG)
loc: Anterior insular cortex, operculo-insular junction (anterior agranular insula)
notes: HIGHLY CHARACTERISTIC insular sign; throat tightening or strangling sensation; Isnard (Lyon SEEG group) key reference; SDE and SEEG-confirmed; must distinguish from non-epileptic events; operculo-insular junction co-activation; often followed by hypermotor behavior
cite: Isnard et al., Ann Neurol 2000; Ostrowsky et al., Neurology 2000; Ryvlin et al. 2006

loc: Anterior insular cortex (primary gustatory cortex, anterior opercular-insular junction)
notes: Metallic, acidic, or bizarre taste; primary gustatory cortex in anterior insula and parietal operculum; SEEG and SDE (Hausser-Hauw & Bancaud) confirmed insular-opercular primacy; stimulation of anterior insula reproduces taste sensations
cite: Isnard et al. 2000; Hausser-Hauw & Bancaud, Brain 1987; Ostrowsky et al. 2000

loc: Anterior insular cortex (interoceptive/visceral representation)
notes: Visceral pain or discomfort from anterior insular interoceptive cortex; overlaps with mesial temporal (epigastric aura) but insular aura often has more burning/pressure quality; SDE/SEEG-confirmed by Isnard group
cite: Isnard et al. 2000; Ostrowsky et al. 2000

loc: Posterior insular cortex (granular insula, posterior third)
notes: Burning, warm, cold thermal quality; SEEG: Ostrowsky (Lyon) showed posterior insula produces thermal and dysesthetic somatosensory sensations; DISTINCT from S1 touch/pressure (S1 = light touch/tingling; posterior insula = thermal/burning/dysesthetic quality); key posterior insular sign
cite: Ostrowsky et al., Neurology 2000; Isnard et al. 2000

loc: Posterior insular cortex / parieto-insular vestibular cortex (PIVC)
notes: True vestibular vertigo from posterior insula/PIVC; Brandt & Dieterich PIVC model; overlaps with TPJ vestibular representation; posterior insula and TPJ form dual vestibular network; cortical stimulation of posterior insula produces vestibular sensations
cite: Brandt et al. 1999; Isnard et al. 2000; Kahane et al. 2003

loc: Posterior insular cortex, opercular cortex (respiratory representation)
notes: Sensation of breathlessness or air hunger; insular/opercular respiratory representation; SEEG: Ryvlin confirmed insular origin of respiratory symptoms; must distinguish from non-epileptic dyspnea or panic; rare but characteristic insular sign
cite: Ryvlin et al. 2006; Isnard et al. 2000

sign: Ictal autonomic cardiac arrhythmia (right insular predominance)
lat: RIGHT > left (right insular for cardiac autonomic)
loc: Right insular cortex (cardiac autonomic representation, predominantly right)
notes: RIGHT insular cortex dominance for cardiac effects; SEEG: Oppenheimer key papers; right stimulation produces arrhythmias more reliably than left; SUDEP mechanism; mandatory cardiac monitoring in all presurgical evaluations
cite: Oppenheimer et al. 1992; Devinsky et al. 2004

loc: Right temporal-insular junction; sympathetic pathway
notes: Sympathetic pilomotor response; ipsilateral to EZ; right-sided predilection (sympathetic right insular); often accompanies fear aura or epigastric aura; Loddenkemper key reference
cite: Loddenkemper et al., Epilepsia 2001; Remillard et al. 1983

sign: Nocturnal hypermotor seizures from insular cortex (mimicking NFLE)
loc: Posterior-superior insula with rapid propagation to frontal premotor/SMA
sens: ~20-30% of apparent NFLE may be insular
notes: EXPERT SIGN: Ryvlin 2006 SEEG study showed insular origin of hypermotor seizures previously attributed to frontal cortex; scalp EEG CANNOT distinguish insular from frontal nocturnal; requires SEEG; posterior-superior insula -> SMA propagation produces hypermotor appearance
cite: Ryvlin et al., Epilepsia 2006; Isnard et al. 2000

sign: Gelastic seizures (ictal mirthless laughter - pathognomonic for hypothalamic hamartoma)
loc: Hypothalamic hamartoma (intrinsically epileptogenic)
notes: PATHOGNOMONIC for hypothalamic hamartoma; ictal mirthless laughter; Berkovic seminal paper; SEEG shows hamartoma generates ictal discharge independently then spreads to fronto-limbic network; MRI confirms diagnosis; may have associated precocious puberty
cite: Berkovic et al., Ann Neurol 1988; Kahane et al. 1994; Striano et al. 2009

sign: Dacrystic seizures (ictal crying - associated with hypothalamic hamartoma)
loc: Hypothalamic hamartoma
notes: Ictal crying associated with hypothalamic hamartoma; often alternates or coexists with gelastic seizures; Kahane SEEG documentation; when present should prompt MRI for hamartoma even without gelastic seizures
cite: Kahane et al. 1994; Striano et al. 2009

sign: Bilateral asymmetric tonic seizure (BATS) at secondary generalization
loc: Motor cortex propagation from temporal or frontal EZ
notes: Both arms tonic with asymmetry at GTCS evolution; extended arm = contralateral to EZ; differs from figure-of-4 (BATS involves both legs elevated + bilateral arm tonic with asymmetry); key pre-GTCS lateralizer
cite: Kotagal et al. 2000; Bleasel et al. 1997

sign: Temporal seizure semiological sequence (orderly progression: aura->arrest->oral->manual->dystonia)
lat: Ipsilateral for each automatism component; contralateral for dystonia
loc: Mesial temporal EZ -> temporal neocortex -> OFC propagation
notes: FRENCH SEEG SCHOOL (Chauvel/Bartolomei/McGonigal): temporal seizures follow orderly semiological sequence encoding propagation; FRONTAL seizures lack this orderly temporal sequence (abrupt hypermotor); sequence timing encodes propagation rate; key diagnostic axis
cite: Chauvel & McGonigal 2014; Bartolomei et al. 2010; Kotagal 1995

sign: Hypermotor vs. hypomotor distinction (primary semiological axis for lobe of origin)
loc: Hypermotor = frontal (mesial/cingulate/OFC/insular); Hypomotor = temporal mesial or bifrontal
notes: PRIMARY DIAGNOSTIC AXIS per Luders semiology classification; hypermotor = frontal (also insular per Ryvlin); hypomotor (arrest/stare) = temporal or bifrontal; SEEG validated across Cleveland and Marseille schools; NOT absolute - SEEG frequently reveals exceptions
cite: Luders & Noachtar 2000; Williamson 1985; Ryvlin et al. 2006; Kotagal 1995

sign: TIRDA (Temporal Intermittent Rhythmic Delta Activity) - scalp EEG interictal
loc: Temporal lobe (ipsilateral temporal scalp electrode T7/T8 or T3/T4)
notes: TIRDA = temporal intermittent rhythmic delta activity; scalp EEG marker; ipsilateral to temporal lobe EZ; highly specific when present; Geyer and Gotman key references; TIRDA > focal temporal slowing in specificity for ipsilateral TLE; correlates with hippocampal sclerosis
cite: Geyer et al. 1999; Normand et al. 1995; Gambardella et al. 1995

loc: Contralateral motor cortex (M1/premotor) or corticospinal pathways
notes: Postictal limb weakness; contralateral to EZ; distinct from Todd paralysis (may be subtle flaccidity); implies motor cortex or adjacent area involvement; useful bedside sign if tested within 2 minutes post-ictally
cite: Bleasel et al. 1997; Gallmetzer et al. 2004

loc: Temporal (mesial temporal + OFC propagation) > frontal
notes: Purposeful-appearing, goal-directed movements; temporal origin produces complex quasi-purposeful automatisms; frontal origin produces stereotyped/hypermotoric patterns without apparent goal; axial orientation of movements provides additional localizing clue
cite: Kotagal 1995; Williamson 1985; Bancaud & Talairach 1992

sign: Axial tonic posturing (trunk extension or flexion)
loc: SMA, premotor cortex, cingulate motor area (bilateral)
notes: Tonic trunk extension or flexion; bilateral axial motor network (SMA + cingulate motor area); frontal lobe seizures; combined with limb asymmetry provides lateralizing information
cite: Williamson et al. 1985; Salanova et al. 1995

loc: Tongue representation in M1 (inferior precentral gyrus, opercular motor area)
notes: Tongue clonic deviation or forced bite; contralateral to EZ; involves motor tongue representation in M1/opercular motor; lateral tongue biting (vs. midline biting) during GTCS strongly lateralizes to EZ; important forensic/medicolegal distinction
cite: Bleasel et al. 1997; Luders & Noachtar 2000
